title: Emergency Care: Cat Gasping for Air or Choking Sounds
meta_desc: Is your cat gasping or choking? Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and how visual triage can help your veterinarian save your cat's life.
content_html:
What Is Respiratory Distress and Choking?

When a cat is gasping for air or making choking sounds, it is experiencing a serious medical crisis known as respiratory distress or dyspnea. This occurs when the body cannot effectively move oxygen into the lungs. You may see your cat stretching their neck out, breathing with an open mouth, or showing exaggerated movements in their chest and abdomen. Unlike dogs, cats rarely breathe through their mouths unless they are in severe physical trouble.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This is a critical emergency. If your cat is gasping or choking, their oxygen levels are likely dropping to dangerous levels, which can lead to organ failure or respiratory arrest within minutes. You should not wait for an appointment or monitor the situation at home. Please transport your cat to the nearest 24-hour emergency veterinary hospital immediately. Keep the car cool and try to keep your cat as calm as possible during transport.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

While your priority is getting to the vet, capturing a 5 to 10-second video of your cat’s breathing can be life-saving if it is safe to do so. Cats often hide their symptoms or change their breathing patterns due to the adrenaline of a car ride or the stress of the clinic environment. Providing a video allows the triage team to see the exact nature of the episode—whether it looks like asthma, a heart-related issue, or a physical obstruction—ensuring they can begin the correct life-saving treatments the moment you arrive.

faqs.question: Could my cat just be coughing up a hairball?
faqs.answer: While hairballs involve retching, the cat usually recovers quickly. If your cat is persistently gasping, has a bluish tint to their gums, or is breathing with an open mouth, it is a medical emergency and not a simple hairball.

faqs.question: Should I try to reach into my cat's mouth to see if they are choking?
faqs.answer: Use extreme caution. A panicking cat may bite reflexively, and you could accidentally push an object further down their throat. Only attempt this if you clearly see an object and can easily sweep it out.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of gasping in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include feline asthma, congestive heart failure (fluid in the lungs), physical obstructions, or severe allergic reactions. All of these require professional diagnosis and oxygen therapy.
